vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Different Categories
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's minclass parameter controls requirements for usage of different character classes, or types, of character that must exist in a password before it is considered valid. For example, setting this value to three (3) requires that any password must have characters from at least three different categories in order to be approved. The default value is zero (0), meaning there are no required classes. There are four categories available: * Upper-case characters * Lower-case characters * Digits * Special characters (for example, punctuation) Modify the minclass setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf entry to require differing categories of characters when changing passwords.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then var_password_pam_minclass='' if grep -sq minclass /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf ; then sed -i "/minclass/d" /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf fi # Strip any search characters in the key arg so that the key can be replaced without # adding any search characters to the config file. stripped_key=$(sed 's/[\^=\$,;+]*//g' <<< "^minclass") # shellcheck disable=SC2059 printf -v formatted_output "%s = %s" "$stripped_key" "$var_password_pam_minclass" # If the key exists, change it. Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'setting2' won't match. if LC_ALL=C g

triage_decision.reason: This finding modifies password complexity requirements via pam_pwquality, a standard STIG hardening measure. The remediation script only edits /etc/security/pwquality.conf and does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, filesystems, or require a reboot. It follows established security policy without introducing lockout risks.

llm_verdict.message: The remediation is complete. The `minclass = 3` parameter has been successfully added to `/etc/security/pwquality.conf`, enforcing password complexity requirements that require at least 3 different character classes. This change follows standard security best practices without affecting authentication mechanisms or requiring a reboot.

llm_verdict.message: The remediation is already complete. The minclass parameter is correctly set to 3 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf, which enforces the password complexity requirements by requiring passwords to have characters from at least 3 different categories. There are no other configuration files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/ that contain minclass settings. The finding has been resolved without any changes needed.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan would resolve the vulnerability by adding minclass = 3 to /etc/security/pwquality.conf, enforcing password complexity requirements. However, the approach is somewhat manual and could be improved by using a more robust configuration management method.
concerns: The plan only mentions modifying /etc/security/pwquality.conf but doesn't address potential duplicate entries or existing commented configurations
No verification step is included to confirm the change was applied correctly
The plan doesn't consider other configuration files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/ that might also need updating
suggested_improvements: Include a grep check before sed to avoid duplicate entries
Add a verification step to confirm the minclass value is set correctly
Consider checking and updating all relevant configuration files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/
Use a more robust configuration management approach like Ansible or Puppet for production environments

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly identifies that the vulnerability is already resolved since minclass is set to 3. This meets the STIG requirement for password complexity. The current state is secure.

suggested_improvements: Add a verification step using grep to confirm minclass=3 is set correctly
Include checks for duplicate entries and remove them if found
Consider checking all configuration files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/
Implement configuration management via Ansible/Puppet for production consistency
